title: From Defects to Devices: Design Guidelines for High-Performance Diamond-Based Solar Cells and Single-Dopant Diodes
abstract: This work establishes key technological guidelines for designing diamond-based optoelectronic devices, derived from a first-principles investigation of two architectures: a PIN junction with a boron-vacancy-boron (BVB) intermediate-band absorber, and a PN junction based on phosphorus-vacancy (PV) defects. For the PIN solar cell, practical design principles include: i) aligning incident light in the xz-plane to exploit anisotropic absorption; ii) using graded junctions to mitigate tunnelling losses at abrupt interfaces; iii) targeting an absorber thickness of ~500 nm to balance absorption and carrier extraction; and iv) leveraging the high transparency of both contact layers for bifacial device configurations. For the PN diode, the PV-doped diamond operates via impurity-band conduction, making it suitable for degenerate p-type applications such as tunnel diodes or asymmetric junctions, while its temperature-dependent Seebeck anisotropy and sign-reversal offer opportunities for thermal management applications. When paired with phosphorus-doped n-type regions, these defects enable single-dopant junctions that significantly simplify device manufacturing. Using density functional theory with GW corrections, Bethe-Salpeter equation calculations and carrier transport modelling coupled to device electrostatics via a Poisson solver, we show that the BVB defect introduces intermediate bands without degrading diamond's high carrier mobility or thermal conductivity, while PV-doping provides high conductivity at room temperature through impurity-band transport. Overall, both defect-engineered systems preserve diamond's superior transport and thermal properties even after doping, offering viable pathways for high-performance diamond optoelectronics. These guidelines provide a practical foundation for fabricating efficient diamond-based photovoltaic and diode devices.
summary: This paper provides a theoretical framework for designing diamond-based solar cells and diodes using engineered defects like BVB and PV. It demonstrates how these defects can enable intermediate-band absorption and simplified manufacturing while preserving diamond's superior thermal and transport properties.
main_problem: Establishing design guidelines for high-performance diamond-based optoelectronic devices, specifically focusing on intermediate-band solar cells and single-dopant diodes.
main_result: The study identifies that BVB defects introduce intermediate bands that enhance sub-bandgap absorption without degrading diamond's transport properties, and that PV-doped diamond enables efficient single-dopant PN junctions.
method: First-principles calculations using DFT with GW corrections for electronic structure, BSE for optical properties, and linearized Boltzmann transport equation for carrier and thermal transport.
model_or_system: Diamond-based architectures including PIN junctions with BVB intermediate-band absorbers and PN junctions based on phosphorus-vacancy (PV) defects.
key_observables: Bandgap, optical absorption spectra, carrier mobility, lattice thermal conductivity, Seebeck anisotropy, and electric field profiles.
important_parameters: BVB defect concentration (target 0.4%), absorber thickness (~500 nm), and solar spectra (AM1.5G and AM0).
main_assumptions: DFT underestimates the bandgap necessitating GW corrections; excitonic effects are assumed to be significantly screened in heavily doped regions; BVB transport calculations were limited to a 4x4x4 supercell.
figures_summary: Schematics of defect environments, visualization of supercells, magnetization vs. temperature, electronic band structures (DFT vs. GW), and analysis of electric field/doping profiles in PIN/PN junctions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of diamond optoelectronics, details the defect-engineered architectures, presents electronic and optical property calculations, discusses transport and thermal properties, and concludes with practical device design guidelines.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2: An example of supercell doped with PV defect. The blue sphere represent the phosphorus atom, while the brown spheres in- dicate the positions of the carbon atoms.

Figure 1: A scheme representing local environments of considered defects: (a) boron-vacancy-boron (BVB) defect, (b) boron and phos- phorus single substitutional defects, and (c) phosphorus-vacancy (PV) defect.

Figure 3: Electronic band structures (BS) of system doped with (a) BVB, (b) B, (c) P, and (d) PV defects. The black and red lines correspond to the DFT and GW-corrected BS. The green arrows represent DFT band gaps reported in Table 3. The Fermi level is set to 0 eV relative to the DFT BS and is indicated by a dashed blue line.
Figure 6: Absorption spectrum α of the BVB absorber region along all three Cartesian directions compared to AM1.5G and AM0 solar spectra.
Figure 4: Absorption coefficients along the x-axis of the pristine (C) and BVB systems, calculated with BSE and GW-corrected RPA.
